chunk_summary: This module-based approach for Indian households addresses overnutrition and obesity through practical lifestyle changes, focusing on reducing liquid calories, improving meal timing, and adopting culturally appropriate plate-based diets.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of reducing liquid calorie intake and ultra-processed snacks in Indian households.
Learn strategies to improve meal timing, including breakfast and dinner routines, and school tiffin modifications.
Implement plate-based diet modifications tailored to Indian cuisine for improved satiety and adherence.
Identify common pitfalls in lifestyle interventions, such as restrictive diets and mismatched dietary recommendations.
key_facts: Sugary beverages should be replaced with water or unsweetened drinks to reduce energy excess.
Daily breakfast and restructuring late dinners are key to improving meal timing.
A balanced plate should include half vegetables, a quarter protein, and a quarter whole grains or millets.
Monitoring methods include tracking beverages per day, meals with screens (yes/no), and plate photos 3 days per week.
Avoid restrictive diets that may trigger rebellion or disordered eating risks.
reference_quote: WHO recommends reducing free sugar intake to less than 10% of total energy intake, with further reduction to below 5% for additional health benefits. (WHO Guideline: Sugars intake for adults and children, 2015)
document_key_topics: Nutrition and meal structure for Indian households
